title: RIFE-Nano-S 270p latency: browser (WebGPU) vs native (DirectML) — rigorous re-measure
input: single fixed deterministic frame pair, byte-identical on both sides (img0.bin/img1.bin)
precision_both_sides: fp32 (model is fp32; neither side was asked to downcast)
readback_both_sides: output read back to CPU on every timed run
timing: warmup K=10 discarded, window N=50, median is the citable value
label: browser onnxruntime-web WebGPU vs native onnxruntime DirectML — NOT vs native-max (a TensorRT path would be faster, so the true browser-vs-best-native gap is larger)

output_parity.verdict: HIGH correlation (0.99999) but NOT bit-identical: peak divergence ~8% of range exceeds pure fp32 rounding, so one pipeline computes some ops differently (likely mixed precision / a different warp/resize kernel in onnxruntime-web WebGPU). The two are visually equivalent but not the same computation.

decision: We do NOT headline this ratio. Reasons: (1) the '73x' it replaced had a migrating provenance (was first described as a memory multiplier, then a speed ratio) — a cargo-cult smell; (2) outputs are not bit-identical (8% peak divergence), so 'apples-to-apples' is qualified, not exact; (3) 'the browser is 41x slower than native' argues against the in-browser products (diciz). What we keep on the landing is the clean, defensible measured fact: native RIFE-Nano runs at ~4 ms/frame (DirectML, RTX 4070) — real-time. No browser-vs-native ratio is published.
native_latency_citable: ~4 ms/frame (median 4.127 ms, p95 4.521 ms, std 0.302 ms, n=50, warmup discarded)
